title: MIRROR, MIRROR
text: MIRROR, MIRROR by Joshua Palmatier A urel Ickerson contained his grief and outrage over the death of his wife, wadding it up like the cloth contained in his pocket and tucking it away, then asked, “Are you ready?” King Rickard Syboll ground his teeth together and nodded. They faced the mirror before them. Aurel raised one hand, palm flat, near the surface but not touching it. As the court’s mirrorwright, he could feel the power of the magically-imbued silvering on the far side of the glass. It prickled against his skin. He bowed his head, muttered an incantation, then reached forward. His hand passed through the glass to the other side. Where the glass intersected the flesh and bone of his wrist beneath his sleeve it felt as if jagged shards were slicing into skin, but he could wiggle his fingers, felt the air on the far side of the pane, colder than here in the throne room. Then he and King Rickard stepped through the mirror. For the space of a breath, no more, that sensation of jagged glass permeated Aurel’s entire body. He caught a glimpse of refracted gray, tasted blood and ash and limestone— Then he and his king emerged into the arbitration room together. It was a close room, walled in stone, the center filled with a massive, elongated black oak table, a chair no more than a pace away from where they stood. Behind them, a mirror stood, the doorway back to Rickard’s throne room. Across the length of the table—adorned with parchment, a quill, ink, sand, a candle, and wax at either end, and nothing else—sat another chair and behind it another mirror. Flames burned in sconces along both walls, in the rounded chandelier overhead. The light refracted in a hundred other mirrors of varying sizes spread along the walls to either side and on the ceiling. The room smelled of stone and paper, lamp oil and char. There were no doors, no windows, but Aurel could sense the power emanating from the mirrors. It throbbed against his skin, potent, dark, and concentrated. Rickard stepped forward, one hand reaching for the back of the chair before them. “Where is the bastard? This is the appointed time—” The mirror on the far side of the room shimmered and two figures emerged. The man on the left, King Ergant Tyroll, was clothed in crimson and gold regalia, a gold-embroidered oak leaf heavy on his chest, bands of gold on his fingers and neck. Yet the sword at his waist was utilitarian, the handle wrapped in worn yet well-oiled leather. When he caught sight of Rickard—in similar finery of blue and silver although, Aurel noted, oddly without his sword—he smirked and said, “Well met, Rickard Syboll. Shall we parley?” Rickard tensed, knuckles whitening in their grip on the chair. He moved around front, steepled his fingers on the table on either side of the stack of parchment, and said, “Let’s talk.” Ergant sidled around his own chair, the two facing each other over the length of the dark oak surface. Not mirrors of each other—Rickard was broader of shoulder, less refined, harsher and blunt—but equals. They sat as one, neither dropping their gaze from the other. Aurel shifted forward to Rickard’s right, yet a step behind, and eyed the woman to Ergant’s left. A mirrorwright as well—she must be—but not one he knew. Not trained at the Arcanum. Her complexion was from the Eastern Isles and white paint dotted her forehead and the skin near her eyes, with swirls of darker tattoos along her throat. The mirrorwright gave him a faint nod of acknowledgment. “I assume you’ve come to apologize for the blatant attacks by your military on the islands along our border—” “ I have come to apologize?” Rickard interrupted, his voice like gravel. “It is you who needs to apologize. Your incursions into Tregard territory— my territory —will no longer be tolerated.” “Incursions? I don’t know of any…incursions.” Rickard slammed a palm flat against the table, the ink, candle, and container of sand jumping with a rattle, but before he could speak, a fifth figure emerged from one of the mirrors midway down the table to their left. Their hands were hidden inside the sleeves of their gray robes, the hood pulled down to shield their face. When they spoke, their voice was genderless. “Rickard Syboll, you have requested arbitration of the Forum. Ergant Tyroll, you have accepted. This chamber has been provided for you, along with food and wine, for as long as it takes for you to reach a mutual agreement so that the Seven Isles can remain at peace.” As they spoke, two more robed figures emerged from mirrors on either end of the table and set decanters of wine, glasses, and trays of assorted fruits, breads, and cheeses on the table, then turned and vanished back through the mirrors. “There will be no blood shed in this room and no one shall leave until an agreement has been reached between the two parties. All are equals here. Is it so agreed?” Rickard stood, leaning toward Ergant in challenge. “It is agreed.” Ergant frowned, his arrogance faltering, but he said, “Agreed.” The robed figure bowed. “So it is agreed.” Then he turned and slid into the mirror behind him. Aurel felt a pulse of power shiver through his flesh as all of the flames in the room guttered as if in a breeze. He twisted and reached with one hand toward the mirror that led to Tregard, to home, felt the prickling of the magic against his palm as before, but when he muttered the incantation and pressed his hand forward it met the cold surface of the glass. He faced Rickard. “They’ve sealed the room.” “Good,” the king said, swiveling toward Ergant. “Then we can finally get justice for what has been done.” “Justice?” Ergant asked. “It is Elleron that seeks justice here, for what you have done.” Rickard snorted. “And what do you believe I have done?” Ergant shifted in his seat, rested his elbows on the table, and clasped his hands together. “Does that mean you are ready to proceed?” Rickard hesitated, then sank back into his chair. “I am.” Ergant reached for his quill, tested the nib, then dipped it in the ink. Hand poised over the first sheet of parchment, he glanced up at Rickard. “Then let us list our grievances.” Snatching up his own quill, Rickard began to write. The scritch-scratch of writing filled the hall, the sound grating in Aurel’s bones. Attempting to ignore it, and the lower thrum of his own anger wadded up deep inside, he glared at the unknown mirrorwright, at Ergant, and focused on his breathing. He’d come as an advisor—and because he was Rickard’s only mirrorwright—not because of Elean and the rest of her family; not because of what had been done to their village of Istabol because of the orders of this madman. Even as he thought it, his hands closed into fists. He tore his gaze away from Ergant and faced the wall of mirrors behind him. His reflection caught his gaze, but almost immediately he turned away. He did not want to see his haggard face, the creases of grief that had been etched around his eyes and mouth, especially when he knew there were mirrors here that would reveal more than mere reality, that would skew it, exaggerate it, enhance it. Not all mirrors were traveling portals. Some were portals to the soul. Eyes closed, he seized control of his breath, forced the tension in his shoulders to ease. His fingers unclenched, one hand reaching into a pocket to grip the damning cloth folded up inside. His thumb caressed the embroidered emblem and his resolve hardened. He returned to his position as Rickard finished writing. The king paused, quill trembling, then he set it aside and blew gently on the parchment. Sand came next, sprinkled so as to absorb any blobs of ink that remained, parchment tapped on its edge to clean it off. Then he sat back, eyes on Ergant as the King of Elleron continued scribbling. Aurel glanced over Rickard’s shoulder at the list of grievances, frowning at how trivial some of them sounded. An instance where Ergant had insulted Rickard’s lineage at a formal gala. A personal slight at the most recent regatta, the competition this year between the isles particular fierce. A few trading contracts that had never been fulfilled. But then he reached the more serious infractions, those that had driven them to the arbitration: the attacks on Tregard’s trading ships along the Elleron border seas; the ships that had simply vanished, all hands and cargo presumed lost; the raids on coastal villages on all of the isles adjacent to Elleron territory. Aurel read through the list of ships, the names of the villages, his anger escalating again, but his anger cut short when he reached the end. He took a step forward involuntarily and Rickard glanced back at the movement. “Aurel?” Aurel shot a look toward the far end of the table, saw the mirrorwright watching him. He straightened, then placed himself at the edge of table, facing Rickard, back to Ergant and his mirrorwright. Rickard frowned up at him. “You’ve forgotten Istabol,” he muttered, and pointed toward the list of towns and villages that had been raided. “On the isle of Kent, your northernmost holding.” Irritation flashed through Rickard’s eyes and he waved a hand. “I haven’t forgotten it. I can’t list every village affected by these ‘raids’ along our coast. We wouldn’t have enough parchment.” Ergant cleared his throat and both Rickard and Aurel looked toward the far end of the table. The king held up his own sheet of parchment. “If you are finished?” Rickard stood, picked up his own sheet, handed it to Aurel. Aurel held it a moment, frowning, until Rickard gestured for him to move. He scanned the list again as he walked down the side of the table, the foreign mirrorwright mirroring him. The list of villages appeared complete. Perhaps a few ports—no more than landings—missing. Nothing as significant as Istabol. They met near the center of the table, face to face, not even an arm’s-length apart. Aurel sucked in a deep breath, smelled some kind of spice, sharp and enticing. The mirrorwright bowed her head formally. “Utak. It is a pleasure to meet a fellow walker.” It took a moment for him to realize Utak was her name, what with the distraction of the missing village and the peculiarity of her accent. “Aurel,” he mumbled, and nodded in return. She smiled and held out Ergant’s grievances. He hesitated, then took the page, handed over Rickard’s list, and headed back toward his king. Rickard snatched it from his hands, gaze flashing over the neat, elegant script. “You call these grievances? These are nothing but complaints, most of them the travails of doing business in the Isles.” “I could say the same of your own list,” Ergant said, tossing Rickard’s paper to the table. “I questioned your lineage? The war for the throne—which your father won—lasted ten years. Rumors that the bastard line prevailed have been circulating ever since.” “That is all that they are. Rumors.” “And the trade deal with the Southern Winds Company fell through because the cargo was never delivered to port.” “I have papers signed by your harbormaster that say it did. You simply refused to pay.” Ergant leaned forward. “Do you have these papers?” Rickard reached into the fold of his formal jacket and Aurel saw Utak tense. Rickard noticed as well, hand stilled. He withdrew a folded piece of parchment with a contemptuous grunt, held it up in plain view. “Simply paper, nothing more.” Utak eased back and nodded. Aurel passed the document to Utak, as before, and retreated. Ergant unfolded it, inspected it and the seal at the bottom closely. “Assuming this is not a forgery, then an apology is in order.” He handed the paper to Utak. “What about my own grievances? You promised a shipment of iron ingots in return for the use of our masons. We have yet to receive it, even though my masons have already begun work.” Rickard sighed. “The ship carrying that cargo was delayed by weather. You know the waters of the straight are vicious this time of year.” Ergant lips thinned in disbelief. “Delayed by four weeks?” “Damaged in a storm and seeking repairs.” The conversation continued, a dance of parry and thrust, but with words. Ground was given and taken. Aurel listened in growing agitation as trivial matters were settled, concessions made, agreements reached. Parchment passed between his and Utak’s hands, signed and sealed with wax. Yet neither king had broached the real grievance yet—the attacks on the trading ships and islands along their border. At an agreed upon break, Aurel asked, “Why have you not brought up the raids upon our villages?” On Istabol, he wanted to say. The raids that had killed Elean and her family, left them butchered, blood soaking into the sands, as the fishermen’s huts burned. “Soon. That is practically all that is left to discuss.” Rickard ripped a few grapes from a clutch and popped them into his mouth as Aurel poured them both a glass of wine. The king’s eyes never left Ergant, narrowed in hunger, as he stabbed at the sliced meat, sopping up juices with bread. “He will not be able to dance his way free of our accusations with words. Reparations will be needed. Tregard will walk away with his southern isles.” Aurel frowned. “What about reparations to those that remain in the villages that were attacked? They lost their homes, their livelihood…loved ones.” Rickard glanced up at him in annoyance. “Of course. That’s what I meant when I said reparations. I haven’t forgotten Istabol. Nor what you lost.” Elean. His wife. Visiting her family, simple fisherfolk, minding their own business, plying the sea, unaware of all of the political maneuvering surrounding their island and the waterways near it. Yet they’d paid the price. Aurel’s fingers tightened on the stem of his glass and he was forced to set it down. He wiped his sweaty hand against his jacket and realized Rickard was watching him closely. “You should try some of the cheese,” his king said. “It’s rather good.” “I’m not hungry.” “Understandable after what happened to your wife.” Before Aurel could respond, Rickard’s gaze flashed toward Ergant’s end of the table. “It would appear we are to begin again.” He stood, Aurel stepping to the side and behind him again. At the far end of the table, Ergant had shoved the tray of food aside. “My mirrorwright has reminded me that we have not yet brought up the attacks upon Tregard’s ships and villages along Elleron’s waters,” Rickard said. “Should we dispense with the trivialities and discuss recompense?” “Ah, yes, these alleged attacks. Why would there be recompense? These raids are obviously the result of pirates. The Isles have always had an issue with brigands. It is part of the risk of doing business on these waters. You know this.” “Because these attacks aren’t being done by pirates, as you well know.” Ergant frowned in consternation. “I don’t know what you’re talking about.” Rickard slammed a fist down on the table. “I have reports from the few ships that managed to escape and the survivors in some of the villages. The ‘pirates’ were too well-organized, too well-equipped. The men claimed they had military experience, that they moved like soldiers.” “Were they wearing Elleron uniforms?” “No.” Aurel shot Rickard a bewildered look, hand clutching at the cloth in his pocket. “Then I fail to see what evidence you have that I had anything to do with it. Soldiers have been known to abandon their posts. It’s not hard to believe some of them became pirates. One of them may have even trained his own band of brigands.” “Don’t lie to me, Ergant. I know you’re the one behind these attacks. I’ve lost seven ships to these ‘pirates.’ Two more barely escaped with their lives and the ship intact. And what of the villages? Argo, Teppit, Grean, Ival, a half dozen more…all of them razed to the ground, the villagers slaughtered. And for what? Nothing was taken but a few meager loads of fish. The villagers had nothing of value. The only thing they had in common was location—along Elleron-Tregard waters!” Ergant was shaking his head. “Elleron had nothing to do with this. I had nothing to do with this.” “And what of Istabol?” Aurel demanded, stepping forward, voice rising. The casualness of Ergant’s lie and ease of his dismissal of the matter infuriated him, overrode ingrained protocol. “What of Istabol, you bastard?” “Istabol?” Ergant appeared honestly confused, glancing down at the now crumpled and worn list of Rickard’s demands, items notated and marked off. “There is no Istabol on this list.” “Istabol! A small island on the edge of our mutual border, tucked into a corner, useless for anything except fishing. Not even on a trade route. And yet you used it as a target. You sent your soldiers there and had them butcher the villagers. They killed everyone, including my wife, her family, the children…everyone.” Aurel had advanced down the length of the table, had halted in the middle by force of habit. He stood there, trembling in rage. “I don’t know anyth—” “Don’t deny it!” Aurel roared, snatching the cloth from his pocket and throwing it onto the table. It landed with the embroidered gold oak leaf face up, on a swatch of torn blood-red fabric. “You can’t deny it! I found this clutched in my wife’s hand where her corpse had been thrown to the sand outside her family’s hut. These weren’t pirates. Your soldiers were there!” Ergant had stilled, his gaze fixed on Aurel. It dropped to the cloth and his brow creased. He began to move around the table, Utak shifting to stop him. He thrust her aside and stalked down its length to stand before Aurel, reaching for the cloth. Utak followed at his heels, her eyes locked on Aurel. Ergant held the swatch in one hand, ran his hand over the embroidery, stained with Elean’s blood. Aurel shook, hands clenched. Ergant met his gaze. “This is part of one of our uniforms, but I swear that I have never heard of Istabol before today.” Something inside Aurel broke. He reached for Ergant with a low growl. He didn’t know what he intended—to gouge out his eyes with his bare fingers, clamp his mouth down on his throat and bite and tear until he tasted royal blood—but Utak stopped him, her hands seizing his wrists as she interposed herself between them. She let him struggle against her and said calmly, “No bloodshed in this room.” He gave a ragged cry and thrust back from her, panting, eyes still locked on Ergant. “He’s lying. We have the proof.” “You have a bloodied cloth,” Utak said. “That is not evidence. That is not truth.” A scream began to well up inside Aurel—built of frustration and pain—and then he caught sight of the mirror beside them. He met Utak’s eyes. “There is one way to find the truth.” Confusion caused her to pause and in that void of uncertainty Aurel muttered an incantation and reached for Ergant. He flung himself to the side, dragging Ergant with him, slipping into the mirror. Comprehension dawned on Utak’s face a moment before they vanished. The world flashed a refracted gray and the taste of blood and ash and limestone coated Aurel’s throat. He staggered back, caught Ergant’s weight against him and held it tight as the sensation of glass slicing across flesh settled over him—over them both, he knew. Ergant bellowed and began to thrash, but Aurel held on fiercely. “Don’t!” he shouted. “It’s too late! You’re already here.” Ergant fought a moment more, then slowed, breath heaving. “Where have you taken me? This isn’t possible. The chamber was sealed.” Aurel released him. “We haven’t left the chamber. We’re inside the glass of the mirror, the space between the chamber and the silvering at its back. But at the same time we’re outside it, in a space between, a world between. You cannot hide here. You cannot lie . The glass allows only the truth.” They stood amidst thousands of floating shards, glittering with reflected silver light, iridescent rainbows forming and vanishing as the shards shifted and rotated with a frisson of sound like ice slowly cracking. It burrowed into Aurel’s skull, juddered in his teeth, made them ache. In the flat surface of one of the shards, he saw Utak turn toward Rickard. He didn’t have much time. Ergant swallowed, faced Aurel. “Why did you bring me here?” “To find out the truth. Did you have anything to do with the attacks on Tregard’s ships?” “I told you, I—” But one of the shards flared and in it Ergant was handing over a purse to the captain of a ship. The captain reached into the pouch and pulled out a fistful of coins, letting them fall back into the sack as behind him his crew broke open a crate and snatched at the cutlasses inside. A few more shards flared, showing similar scenes, different captains, different ships, Elleron soldiers drilling the pirates in sword work, in attack patterns, in strategy. Farther away, more shards depicted those same ships on the open sea, hunting other ships. Cannons fired with bursts of fire and smoke. Hulls were breached, decks boarded. Tradesmen were gutted without mercy. Blood flowed like the sea. Ergant’s breath caught at the images, until he faced Aurel again. “I contacted certain pirates and struck a deal—money and weapons and training in exchange for the targeting of Tregard trade vessels.” “What about the villages?” Ergant shook his head, but the shards had already reacted, the images shifting to the raids. Aurel took a step towards one for a better look, breath held, thinking it might be Istabol. One of the rotating shards sliced across his arm, cutting through cloth and skin, edge as fine as paper. Blood welled. The sting was sharp, like a papercut, but not as unsettling as the sensation of jagged slashes that came from entering this world, steadily sinking deeper, into muscle, moving toward bone, as if he were being flayed alive. His gaze jumped from shard to shard, watched fisher families being hauled from their huts, children crying, women shrieking, many breaking free to run as the pirates began to cut them down. “I—” Ergant’s jaw clenched. “I never told them to hit the villages. They did that on their own.” “But you were complicit. You didn’t stop them once it started.” Ergant sidestepped some of the shards. “No, I didn’t. Even after reports reached me of what they were doing.” Aurel moved toward him, sudden and intent. “What about Istabol?” Ergant shook his head. “I know nothing—” “What about Istabol!” Aurel spun away from Ergant, searched the shards, but they were all blank, reflecting only this shattered world and their own faces. And then, abruptly, Utak appeared, shoving a struggling Rickard before her. Aurel’s king stumbled and fell to the ground composed of slivers of glass, cried out as they sliced into his hands, his knees. He caught himself, then staggered upright, hands shaking, splinters of glass jutting from his palms. A floating shard nicked his neck and he lurched to the side with another cry. “What is this place?” he gasped, gaze jerking around at the shards, at the blood dripping from his hands, at Ergant and Aurel. When they landed on Utak, he roared, “What is this place!” Utak ignored him, stepping up to Aurel’s chest. “How dare you bring my liege to T’giskall . It is a sacred place, a hellish place, not to be tread by men.” Rickard had limped to Aurel’s side, hands held protectively before him. Blood had begun to stain his breeches at the knees and Aurel could now see shards embedded there as well. Yet Rickard straightened, gathered dignity and arrogance and rage around him like a cloak. “What is she talking about, Aurel?” “We aren’t supposed to be here,” Aurel answered. “We were never meant to stay, only pass through when using the mirror portals. That was the agreement. Few of the mirrorwrights are even aware of this place.” Then he spoke to Utak. “But it was the only way to know the truth.” Utak’s expression hardened. “And have you found your truth?” Aurel glanced at Ergant. “I have. He arranged for the attacks on our trading ships. They were done by pirates, but in the employ of Elleron, including the raids on our ports and villages.” “I knew it!” Rickard tilted his head back and laughed, then pointed a bloody finger at Ergant. “I knew you were behind it, even though I couldn’t prove it. There was no evidence. But now you will pay. You will pay with land and goods and trade lanes. Tregard will own the southern Isles!” Something sounded beneath the steady hiss of breaking glass—a crackle, as of glass scraping against glass; a crunch, as of a mirror stepped on underfoot. Utak reached out to grip Aurel’s arm, fingers so tight his skin turned white. “We have to go.” But Aurel had latched onto what Rickard had said, his focus now on his king. “No evidence? But what of the oak leaf cloth I found in Elean’s hand?” Rickard pulled back from Ergant, his expression flickering from shock to vacancy, eyes shuttered closed. “I—it was—the Tregard soldiers—” The shards around him flared and Aurel shifted closer in order to see. The first showed Istabol, the villagers interrupting their daily chores as strangers entered the village. But not from the sea. These men came from inland. Soldiers. Tregard soldiers. In the next, the villagers were being summoned to the village docks, gathered into a ragged clump, confused, the bewilderment shifting to anger as the soldiers became rough, dragging a pregnant woman from her hut and tossing her to the ground at the edge of the group. Aurel glanced at the next shard and the next, the story unfolding in snippets of action and flares of light. Elean stepping from the crowd in affront, demanding to know what was happening, the soldiers’ commander ignoring her. He could imagine her declaring who she was, her importance to the court as the mirrorwright’s wife. But the commander didn’t falter. Elean’s parents came forward, but she motioned them back, following the commander, hounding him, as the last of the villagers were pulled from their boats and herded into the group already gathered. And then the soldiers all drew their swords—cutlasses, not the standard short swords—and the slaughter began. The commander ran his sword up into Elean’s gut, his wife gasping, one hand reaching for the commander’s shoulder, the other clutching at the sword impaling her. She heaved once, blood bubbling up out of her mouth. Then the commander pulled the blade free and turned away before she fell. Something hard and jagged and hot lodged itself in Aurel’s throat. He tried to swallow, but it felt as if he were swallowing glass. Elean crumpled to the sand, chest heaving as she cradled her stomach, and then she stilled. Around her the soldiers cut their way through the villagers with ease. Almost no one resisted. No one had the chance. The butchery ended in moments. The commander surveyed the scene, ordered soldiers to search the huts again, the surrounding trees, the boats. Rickard appeared and sauntered up to his commander’s side. Aurel’s king stared down at Elean’s body, then knelt and pulled a tattered swath of red cloth from his jacket. It fluttered in the breeze from the ocean, the gold oak leaf clear. He wadded up the cloth and grabbed Elean’s limp arm, closing her fingers around it. They must have still been warm, her blood still fresh. Then Rickard placed her arm back onto the sand—staged it—and stood. The shard rotated so the scene was no longer in view. Aurel faced Rickard. “You killed Elean.” His voice ground out in splinters. Rickard stiffened in defiance. “The mirror lies.” “Not here,” Aurel said. The crackle-crunch sounded again, louder this time, closer, and Utak’s eyes widened, the whites stark against her skin. “Aurel!” But Aurel was focused on Rickard. He took a step closer. “You did it to frame Ergant, to force his hand. Did you even know Elean was there?” The shards flared, but Aurel didn’t need to look to know the truth. “No, you chose Istabol because Elean was there. Because you knew I’d be destroyed, that I wouldn’t let it go . That’s why you didn’t write down Istabol on your list—because you knew it wasn’t Ergant’s fault. You knew I wouldn’t be able to leave it alone, that I wouldn’t let such an ‘oversight’ slide. You wanted me angry, off-balance. And that’s why you didn’t bring your sword. I was to be your weapon.” “Do you think Istabol was safe?” Rickard asked. “It could have been attacked by Ergant’s pirates as easily as any of the other villages. It could have been next! He needed to be stopped.” “He would have been. Eventually.” “He needed to be stopped now ! Before his marauders hit a more important target.” Aurel stilled and the crackle-crunch filled the silence, a regular sound now, steady, like the slow approach of footsteps, feet shuffling through broken glass. “More important than Elean?” Aurel asked. Realization dawned on Rickard’s face, but Utak had stepped up to Aurel’s side. In a hard whisper, she said, “Aurel, look.” He glanced toward where she nodded, already knowing what he would see. Figures were approaching, mere shadows among the gray in the thickness of the mirror, but edging closer. Shadows made of shards and splinters and ground glass. Shadows with empty black pits for eyes, a tiny spark at their center. Shadows that had already closed half the distance between them. Behind him, Ergant sucked in a hollow breath and stepped toward the other three. “What are they?” “The R’skall ,” Utak said. “The residents of this realm,” Aurel added. Utak faced him, terror in her eyes. “They are unhappy. They want blood.” Aurel glanced down at the slice the shard had cut into his arm, at Rickard’s hands and knees. He met Rickard’s eyes. “They shall have it.” “No,” Rickard said. Aurel motioned to Utak. “Get Ergant out of here.” Utak snatched at Ergant’s hand, already mumbling the incantation. Her gaze met Aurel’s as she stepped to the side, pulling Ergant with her, both of them vanishing into thin air, replaced by one of the R’skall not ten paces away. Others weren’t far behind, closing in on all sides. “You can’t do this,” Rickard hissed in fear. “No blood can be shed in this room!” “We are in a liminal space, both within the room and outside of it. The rules of the Forum don’t apply here.” Rickard’s began shaking. His face transformed into rage. “I am your liege! You swore fealty to me !” “And you swore to protect those who served you.” Aurel began the incantation. Rickard spun to face the R’skall hemming him in, mere steps away. “No,” he heaved. “You can’t leave me here! You can’t abandon me —!” But Aurel stepped to one side, Rickard still in view, his scream building into a howl of pure terror and pain as the R’skall reached for him with jagged arms, enfolding him, glass piercing skin, gouging into organs. Blood spurted. Flesh flensed from muscle and bone. Aurel felt one of the R’skall snag his own arm, the pain frigid and hideous, and then that world grayed and he fell backwards, the R’skall’s hand shredding skin and muscle from upper arm to elbow before letting go. He stumbled backwards out of the mirror, lower back slamming into the black oak table. His left arm shrieked in agony, but he ground his teeth together, breath harsh, and staggered upright. Blood dripped from his fingers and pieces of glass stuck out of his tattered flesh and torn shirt. Each shard burned with cold. Utak took a step forward from where she waited, but halted at the sight of his arm. “They touched you.” “It’s nothing. Our healers will deal with it when I return.” Already, the pain had diminished to a deep throb, his arm going numb with the cold. “You must get every splinter of glass out,” Utak warned. But Aurel ignored her, focused on Ergant, the King of Elleron standing beyond Utak at his end of the table. He tilted his chin up when he caught Aurel’s look. “Is Rickard dead?” “He is.” Aurel rested his wounded hand on the table. “We know the truth of matters now. Tregard demands recompense.” Ergant huffed. “Your king is dead. This arbitration is over.” He reached for the already-agreed-upon papers, signed by both kings, but Aurel said, “Shall we pay T’giskall another visit?” Ergant stilled and Utak shot Aurel a disapproving glance. She knew that he would have to touch Ergant to pull him into the mirror realm, but she said nothing. Ergant’s hand trembled, but he snatched it back. “You are merely a mirrorwright. You cannot speak for Tregard!” “We are all equals here. Here, I am king. And no one can leave until both sides come to an agreement.” Ergant glowered, expression pinched, as if he were chewing on something bitter. “What is it that you want?” “Your southern isles, for a start.” Aurel turned, his hand sliding from the table, leaving behind a handprint and smear of blood. He shifted to the Tregard end of the table, faced Ergant again across its length. Utak remained at its center. “The current locations of these pirate ships you’ve hired as well. I’m certain we can reach an agreement on additional terms.” He sat, reached for the quill, eyes never leaving Ergant’s face. “Shall we parley?” “The hardest working writer in horror” is a phrase which is tossed around much, (maybe too much), but I can't think of another writer who embodies this better than Mark Allan Gunnells. Every time I log onto Facebook, it seems like Mark has either won another flash fiction contest, or published another book, or sold another story. His passion for horror and storytelling is undeniable, and is evident in every single thing he writes, as it is in his offering for this anthology, “Phoenix.”
